skill.name: omakase-off
skill.description: Guides build and implementation requests through brainstorming or parallel exploration of multiple architectural approaches, then compares variants with tests and review.
skill.summary: Explore uncertain implementation approaches in parallel and choose the best tested result.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command and weak-crypto patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown backticks, dependency names, and ordinary workflow text. The confirmed risk is operational: the skill instructs agents to create worktrees, write plans, and force-delete loser branches during cleanup. No evidence found of malicious network use, credential access, obfuscation, or prompt injection aimed at bypassing audit rules.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Git Cleanup Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill workflow tells the agent to always clean up losing variants and provides git commands that remove worktrees and force-delete branches. This is legitimate for variant exploration, but it can delete user work if branches or worktrees contain unmerged changes and the agent proceeds without clear confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cleanup behavior is explicitly documented, including force branch deletion with git branch -D. The intent is workflow cleanup rather than malware, so the risk is moderate operational data loss rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Broad Filesystem Modification Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to create plans, variant directories, worktrees, and result files as part of implementation exploration. These writes are expected for the workflow, but they expand the change surface and require repository isolation and review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file and worktree paths are clearly specified in the skill documentation. The behavior is consistent with a development workflow, but it has meaningful impact on the repository filesystem.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Mostly Markdown References
low_findings.description: Most external command detections occur on backticked skill names, file paths, example labels, or prose in Markdown. These are not executable scripts and do not create command injection by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is documentation text and table entries, not runnable code. The only concrete shell commands found are git workflow commands documented for worktree setup and cleanup.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography And Network Matches Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptography and network reconnaissance indicators, but the reviewed lines contain workflow prose, examples, or dependency references. No evidence found of cryptographic operations, HTTP calls, port scanning, or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations do not contain cryptographic code or network code. Confidence is high because both scanned files are Markdown-only skill instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Force Branch Deletion Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The cleanup example includes git branch -D for loser variants. This can discard branch state if used before verifying that work is unneeded and backed up.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicitly present in a cleanup code block. The danger is accidental data loss, not hidden execution.

content.value_statement: Unclear architecture choices can slow build requests and lead to weak decisions. This skill structures brainstorming, parallel variants, scenario testing, and review so teams can compare real implementations.
actual_capabilities: Offers a choice between guided brainstorming and parallel exploration before detailed implementation.
Detects architectural indecision during planning and proposes variant exploration.
Defines a directory structure for shared design notes, variant plans, and final results.
Coordinates multiple implementation variants through parallel agents or fallback workflows.
Requires scenario testing, fresh review, and a judge-style comparison before selecting a winner.
Documents results and cleanup steps after a winning approach is selected.
limitations: It is a workflow guide, not an executable automation script.
It depends on other skills when available and uses fallback behavior when they are absent.
It can increase repository changes because several variants may be implemented.
Cleanup commands require careful review to avoid deleting useful branch work.

use_cases.title: Compare Architecture Choices
use_cases.description: Explore options such as storage engines, authentication models, or API styles before committing to one implementation.

use_cases.title: Reduce Planning Deadlock
use_cases.description: Move forward when several choices seem plausible by testing real variants instead of debating abstract tradeoffs.

use_cases.title: Evaluate AI Coding Variants
use_cases.description: Use scenario tests and review checkpoints to compare outputs from multiple implementation agents.

prompt_templates.title: Start With A Build Request
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to build a small CLI todo app. Help me choose between brainstorming together and exploring several approaches in parallel.

prompt_templates.title: Explore One Unclear Decision
prompt_templates.prompt: I am not sure whether this feature should use JSON files or SQLite. Use omakase-off to compare the approaches with tests.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Parallel Variants
prompt_templates.prompt: Create parallel implementation plans for two authentication approaches, define shared scenarios, and prepare a comparison workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Judge Completed Variants
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the completed variants, compare scenario results and code review findings, then recommend the winner and cleanup steps.

output_examples.input: Build a dashboard, but I am unsure which frontend state approach to use.
output_examples.output: The assistant offers brainstorming or parallel exploration, identifies state management as the main decision, and proposes two tested variants.

output_examples.input: Try both SQLite and JSON storage for this CLI tool.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates separate variant plans, runs the same scenarios against each result, and summarizes which approach fits better.

output_examples.input: Both implementations are done. Which one should we keep?
output_examples.output: The assistant compares tests, scenario results, review findings, and fitness criteria before recommending a winner.

best_practices: Keep variant count small and focus on the decision with the largest architectural impact.
Run the same scenario tests against every variant before comparing outcomes.
Confirm cleanup actions before deleting worktrees or branches.
anti_patterns: Do not explore every possible combination when one primary decision is enough.
Do not replace scenario testing with manual inspection alone.
Do not delete losing branches until useful changes have been reviewed.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It helps an AI assistant explore multiple implementation approaches in parallel and choose a tested winner.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it when a build request has meaningful architecture uncertainty and several reasonable approaches exist.

faq.question: Does it write code by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides a workflow for planning, dispatching, testing, reviewing, and comparing implementation variants.

faq.question: Why does it mention git worktrees?
faq.answer: Worktrees isolate each variant so separate implementations can be built and tested without mixing changes.

faq.question: Is it safe for production repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. Review all generated changes and confirm branch cleanup before deleting any work.

faq.question: Does it require other skills?
faq.answer: It can use related brainstorming, testing, review, and branch-finishing skills, but it describes fallback steps.
